title: Prologue to War
rights: public domain (presumed; pre-1963 broadcast, no rights statement on archive.org item)
transcript: where I was sitting in my office which was just around the corner from the Wilhelm Strasser it was shortly before midnight that I remember very well in those days we worked very late hours very often till three or even four in the morning and of German news agency came on a telephone with the usual offering about hair collega and then I have a most important announcement to make. But I think then it was midnight and I had the radio turned on and it came over almost simultaneously on the radio. So then I picked up the telephone, I got on to London, think about 10 minutes, gave him the first news, then sat down to write a little thing piece. Then all the British correspondence and American correspondence started telephoning each other and discussing the development. It was too late at night, I think, for there to be really to be much excitement in the streets. Certainly, I didn't notice any elation or duvelation. I think people were both bewildered and concerned. It struck them as quite extraordinary that suddenly out of the blue, they should find themselves allied to the Bolsheviks. They were conducted in a quite informal way, not a large gathering, only five people present, Molotov sat at his desk on a dais raised above the floor. The rest of us were on the Biloium on chairs in a kind of semi-circle, round him, it was very inconvenient. We had to hold our papers on our knees and take our notes on our knees. There was a table in the room, but no one ventured to suggest you had strung the table. There was one rather mysterious little circumstance, Maltoff seemed from time to time to fiddle with what I thought was a switch under his desk, and I assumed that there was a tape recorder somewhere or a microphone, and he was switching in on any passage he thought perhaps ought to be put on a permanent record. He was quite amenable on the whole, very stiff, very rigid, very unyielding, but first to the conversations, he was quite amiable. Towards the end the atmosphere changed, he got very cool and the atmosphere became rather uneasy. We knew that they were in touch with the Germans, they more or less set so, from time to time, publicly, they were talking to the Germans. We did not know exactly what was happening, though we suspected that something rather important was in the wind between them. But that didn't make the impact of the news any less serious. It was obviously a new political reverberant of the very highest importance. obviously changed the situation in the world at large very materially. I think in the cabinet they had a kind of hope that the Hitler would look back into history and say well the British never do default on their main obligations and they would believe and I think correctly believe that Hitler very much did not want to find himself at war with Great Britain. I think a lot of evidence to show that he felt that there was really no reason why there should be a war with Great Britain, and I remember seeing a minister, a man in the German propaganda department in Berlin, said to me quite openly, why don't you British let us expand eastward, leave us alone, to go eastward? And I suppose we thought that if we made this civic statement and you'll remember that it was reiterating in the firmest manner in Parliament in a very famous debate that we would stand by Poland, that we hoped that he would take twice. There was an enormous amount of army transport going through Berlin, heading east. But there were other things you couldn't get petrol. Eggs suddenly disappeared, butter, pastures, coffee. Then they began to put up anti-aircraft guns on some of the big factories. We had a very lengthy life. There was only five British correspondents left where we went to was a never hindrance at the British Embassy and he said, I think you'd better be going. So we just said our farewells, because my home was there. I had my motor car, but I agreed to go by train with the others, and we all met at the platform in the evening. I took I think three suitcases, that was the lot. My car I left outside, I just switched the engine off, put the key in my pocket and walked out. It's all you could do. I was awakened about half past eight by shouting which seemed to be coming from just outside my window. The window was wide open because of the summer. Just below there was a scaffolding. And there was a painter on the scaffolding and he was shouting down to his mates on the pavement down below. Absolutely unmentionable curses. His mates were paying no attention to him but looking up to the sky. I was wondering what it was all about. The door of my room opened and in came the floor waiter, who strode over to the window and banged it shut. And turned to me and said, don't you know that windows must be kept shut during air raids? I looked quite blankly at him and he saw that I didn't understand it. He said, don't you know the war has begun? And then it all dawned on me. This poor painter was on the scaffolding suspended between Heaven and Earth. the left was on, his mates down below were not doing anything about pulling the ropes to get him down. I'd finally decided, after yes, no, yes, no, for a few hours, that I was going to send them. Well, I got them all ready, and after I'd let them go, I'd had a few tears, naturally. But finally, I'd just sit in all the sudden saying, no, I won't let them go. I just cannot be separated from them. I rushed down the stairs and along when I met my husband coming back, well I sent him rushing back to get them and he just took them out of the line and brought them back with their big tickets on, you know, they were all marked where they were going because it was a big thing. I mean it wasn't the only school going, there was every school round about where I was at about ten schools there, well I brought them back and I've thought that I'd rather not let them be separated from me by being evacuated, that if the travel came, I'd tune around them with me, so I decided the only possible way of getting them out of London was to take them hot picking with me. So the EastEnders, well, they just leave in their hundreds of thousands every hot picking time. So off we went. I got to pick a deal about four and there were all those assholes there with the sandbags going on all around the circus and around Eros, and we packed out our flares onto our baskets and we commenced our work. We used to get crowds up watching the advertisement you know in the lights, it was ever so gay, it was people going to theatres and restaurants, you know, we used to do a nice trade in the evening time. Well, this particular day, whether the people where it or whatever was happening but we didn't do much trade so we just sat there and waited for the blackout was fully expecting it we wondered what it was going to be like you know if it was going to be as dark as we expected it to be anyway there was no that when it did eventually come there was no not a lot to be seen not a jingle light and so of course all we had to do was to pack up hair, flowers again, and found our way under this way we could, the neon lighting and the advertising that had all gone. We didn't seem that picketly surface at all. I had made up my mind to leave because I had this question to do so, and there was nothing more that I could do. The Senate sent two members as a guest out there to the Council general. And I was a little startled when they picked their heels and said that the senator told them to come and take me under their protection, which was the usual phrase used in Germany, before people were sent to concentration camps. However, they did go out and look for motor transport, which in fact they didn't find. Eventually, we got my pre-accessors in Metacar, and left Danzig about 7 o'clock in the evening. The American Council General was there to see us off, and there was a good deal of noise going on at the time because the Germans were attacking the post office, and it was defending itself Just before we left, the single individual turned up out of the doom and said he was a British seamen, and how could he get out of dancing? He must have been the last British object left, and I said, will you better come with us, but the Gestaltman said, no, we will see your papers first, and he hadn't got any papers. So he had to be left behind, and what happened to him, I don't know. worry that day was that things didn't seem to be moving as quickly as they should have. Twenty-four hours since the Germans invaded Poland and nothing had happened in England. And that was all reflected in the feeling of the House of Commons. Of course, the whole place was packed, the public area, the Piers Gallery, and all the rest. I remember the prime minister coming in every eye turning to him. Then he got up to speak. What the house wanted and what the country wanted was a statement from the Prime Minister. They didn't get it. All he got was a remark from him that we had received no reply from Germany to our communication when they invaded Well, that just about did it. By protocol, of course, the next remark was to come from the need of the opposition. Well, Mr. Antley wasn't there, I think he was ill. And it was Arthur Greenwood, a very old favorite. And he got up to speak. As soon as he got to his feet, there was a shout, a fierce, impatient shout from the conservative benches. speak for England, it was Leo Amory, and the whole house chaired, and Arthur Greenwood said, how long were we prepared to vacillate, at a time when Britain and all that Britain stood for was in peril. Virtually he gave Chamberlain 12 hours more to make a statement, neither more nor less, and that was the note on which we went to bed that night. I was on duty outside number 10 Downing Street that morning. It was just like a normal day, except that I had my tin helmet and gas mask. I had no special instructions. Of course there was a bit more coming and going. The crowd began to gather about eight o'clock, but never more than four to five hundred people. Much less than on other special occasions. It was all just the same. The milkman delivered the milk at number 10. Then he went across to the foreign office. As he always did. I remember those last two hours went very slowly. There was constant coming and going. Diplomats, ministers, officials of all kinds. Reporters and cameraman began to gather. It was obvious something big was happening. We knew there was a cabinet meeting. I can remember the church bell rang out for Sunday service, and then they'd been struck. Suddenly, the program was interrupted by an announcer shouting, England declares war. And then they've played God save the king. And this news, of course, was immediately spread all over the city by the loudspeakers in the streets by people who'd been listening in their homes. And at once the streets filled with poles, streaming out of their houses and all of them making with one cry to the British Embassy. The Embassy was in one of the main streets in Warsaw, the Novice Viat, and the streets leading to the Novice Viat to where it once filled with a milling crowd of poles, weeping with joy, shouting, I've never seen such an explosion of joy as on that Sunday morning in Warsaw.
